Heart Atlas

A visual reference of cardiac anatomy: the heart in the thorax, external and internal surfaces, coronary vessels, valves, chambers, and the conduction system.

Section 1

The Heart in the Thorax

Where the heart sits, what surrounds it, and the great vessels arriving and leaving.

Plate 01

The Heart in the Mediastinum

The heart sits in the middle compartment of the thoracic cavity, the mediastinum, flanked by the lungs and resting on the diaphragm.

Identify These Structures
Thoracic Cavity
The chest cavity bounded by the rib cage, sternum, vertebrae, and diaphragm. Contains the lungs, heart, and great vessels.
Mediastinum
The central compartment of the thoracic cavity between the two pleural cavities. Contains the heart, great vessels, trachea, esophagus, thymus, and major nerves.
Clinical Pearl
Mediastinal Anatomy on Imaging
On a chest X-ray or CT, the mediastinum appears as the central shadow between the two lungs. A widened mediastinum on a trauma X-ray raises immediate concern for traumatic aortic injury. Mediastinal masses, depending on their compartment, suggest different pathologies: anterior masses commonly point to thymoma, teratoma, thyroid, or lymphoma (the four T words).
Plate 02

Heart in Situ with Pericardium

In its native position, the heart is wrapped in the fibrous pericardium and the great vessels are surrounded by the thymus, which becomes fatty in adulthood.

Identify These Structures
Thymus (Fatty Remnant)
Lymphoid organ active in childhood for T-cell maturation; involutes after puberty leaving fatty tissue in the anterior superior mediastinum.
Ascending Aorta
First segment of the aorta, arising from the left ventricle. Gives off the coronary arteries before becoming the aortic arch.
Pulmonary Trunk
Vessel arising from the right ventricle; carries deoxygenated blood toward the lungs before splitting into right and left pulmonary arteries.
Right Auricle
The ear-shaped pouch projecting from the right atrium; visible anteriorly, draping over the aorta.
Right Atrium
Receives deoxygenated blood from the body via the SVC, IVC, and coronary sinus. Lies underneath and behind the right auricle.
Right Ventricle
Pumps deoxygenated blood to the lungs via the pulmonary trunk. Forms most of the anterior surface of the heart.
Left Ventricle
Pumps oxygenated blood to the body via the aorta. Forms the apex and the left lateral border of the heart.
Fibrous Pericardium
Tough outer sac of dense connective tissue enclosing the heart. Anchored to the diaphragm and great vessels; prevents overfilling.
Clinical Pearl
Cardiac Tamponade
The pericardial sac normally holds 15 to 50 mL of serous fluid. Rapid accumulation of fluid or blood (from trauma, aortic dissection, or post-MI free wall rupture) compresses the heart and prevents diastolic filling. Becks triad (hypotension, distended neck veins, muffled heart sounds) signals tamponade. Treatment: emergent pericardiocentesis. Slow effusions can stretch the pericardium to hold over a liter without tamponade.
Plate 03

Great Vessels Above the Heart

Above the heart, the brachiocephalic veins join to form the SVC on the right while the aorta arches over to the left. The cadaveric and color-coded model views show the same arrangement.

Identify These Structures
Right Brachiocephalic Vein
Formed by the union of the right internal jugular and right subclavian veins; joins the left brachiocephalic to form the SVC.
Left Brachiocephalic Vein
Formed by the union of the left internal jugular and left subclavian veins; crosses the midline to join the right brachiocephalic.
Superior Vena Cava (SVC)
Formed by the union of the two brachiocephalic veins; returns deoxygenated blood from the upper body to the right atrium.
Brachiocephalic Trunk
First branch of the aortic arch; divides into the right common carotid and right subclavian arteries.
Aorta and Aortic Arch
Largest artery of the body. Ascending aorta becomes the arch, which gives off (in order) the brachiocephalic trunk, left common carotid artery, and left subclavian artery before continuing as the descending thoracic aorta.
Pulmonary Vein
Returns oxygenated blood from the lungs to the left atrium. Four pulmonary veins enter the LA, two from each lung.
Right Coronary Artery
Arises from the right aortic sinus; supplies the right side of the heart and (in most people) the inferior wall and AV node.
Left Coronary Artery
Arises from the left aortic sinus; quickly bifurcates into the LAD and circumflex arteries.
Anterior Interventricular Artery (LAD)
Branch of the left coronary running in the anterior interventricular sulcus toward the apex.
Circumflex Artery
Branch of the left coronary running in the left coronary sulcus around to the posterior heart.
Great Cardiac Vein
Runs alongside the LAD anteriorly, then around with the circumflex; drains into the coronary sinus.
Clinical Pearl
Central Venous Access
Central lines are most often placed via the internal jugular or subclavian vein, which drain through the brachiocephalic veins into the SVC. The catheter tip should sit at the SVC-right atrium junction. Subclavian access carries pneumothorax risk (the apex of the lung sits just below); jugular access carries a small risk of carotid puncture. SVC syndrome from a lung tumor compressing the SVC produces facial swelling, distended neck veins, and headache.
Plate 04

Posterior Mediastinal Vessels and Airways

Behind the heart sit the trachea, esophagus, and major vessels. The trachea bifurcates at the carina; the esophagus runs immediately posterior, descending to pierce the diaphragm.

Identify These Structures
Trachea
Airway extending from the larynx (C6) to the carina (T4/T5), where it bifurcates into right and left mainstem bronchi. Lies anterior to the esophagus.
Esophagus
Muscular tube extending from the pharynx through the diaphragm at T10. Lies immediately posterior to the trachea and left atrium.
Right Mainstem Bronchus
Wider, shorter, and more vertical than the left; aspirated objects more often lodge here.
Right Subclavian Artery
Arises from the brachiocephalic trunk; passes laterally toward the right upper limb.
Right Subclavian Vein
Joins the right internal jugular vein to form the right brachiocephalic vein.
Left Subclavian Artery
Third branch of the aortic arch; passes laterally toward the left upper limb.
Left Subclavian Vein
Joins the left internal jugular vein to form the left brachiocephalic vein.
Right Pulmonary Veins
Two veins (superior and inferior right) draining the right lung; enter the left atrium on its right side.
Left Pulmonary Veins
Two veins (superior and inferior left) draining the left lung; enter the left atrium on its left side.
Clinical Pearl
The Esophagus Behind the Left Atrium
Because the esophagus sits directly behind the left atrium, transesophageal echocardiography (TEE) can image cardiac structures with extraordinary clarity. TEE is particularly useful for visualizing the LA appendage to rule out thrombus before cardioversion in atrial fibrillation, for assessing prosthetic valves, and for diagnosing aortic dissection. Catheter ablation of LA arrhythmias near the posterior wall carries a rare but devastating complication: atrioesophageal fistula.
Section 2

External Heart Anatomy

Surface views, model orientations, and the grooves that mark internal boundaries.

Plate 05

Surface Landmarks: Apex, Base, and Chambers

The model orients the heart in the body: the broad base sits superiorly toward the great vessels, and the pointed apex aims inferiorly and to the left.

Identify These Structures
Base of the Heart
The broad superior surface formed mainly by the left atrium. Where the great vessels enter and leave; oriented toward the right shoulder.
Apex of the Heart
The pointed inferior tip, formed by the left ventricle. Aims down, forward, and to the left; lies at about the 5th left intercostal space.
Right Atrium
Receives deoxygenated blood from the systemic circulation and coronary sinus.
Right Auricle
Muscular flap projecting from the right atrium; increases atrial volume.
Right Ventricle
Anterior chamber; pumps blood through the pulmonary trunk to the lungs.
Left Auricle
Muscular flap projecting from the left atrium; visible on the anterior left side.
Left Ventricle
Forms the apex and the left lateral border. Pumps blood through the aorta to the systemic circulation. Wall is about three times thicker than the right ventricle.
Clinical Pearl
The Apex Beat (PMI)
The point of maximal impulse (PMI) is normally palpable at the 5th intercostal space, just medial to the midclavicular line. Displacement laterally or inferiorly suggests cardiomegaly or left ventricular dilation. A heave (sustained PMI) suggests left ventricular hypertrophy. The apex beat is the cardiac landmark most commonly assessed in physical exam.
Plate 06

The Anatomical Model: Three Label Sets

The same anterior model view is shown three times with different labels, isolating the venous, arterial, and chamber/airway anatomy in turn.

Identify These Structures
Internal Jugular Veins
Drain the brain via the sigmoid sinuses; descend lateral to the carotid arteries; join subclavian veins to form brachiocephalic veins.
External Jugular Veins
Drain the scalp and face superficially; cross sternocleidomastoid before joining the subclavian veins.
Subclavian Veins
Continue from the axillary veins; receive the external jugular veins; combine with internal jugulars to form brachiocephalic veins.
Brachiocephalic Veins (R and L)
Form behind the sternoclavicular joints; merge to form the SVC. The left crosses the midline anterior to the great arteries.
Anterior Cardiac Veins
Small veins on the anterior right ventricle; drain directly into the right atrium, bypassing the coronary sinus.
Great Cardiac Vein
Begins at the apex, ascends with the LAD, then turns left in the coronary sulcus to become the coronary sinus.
Common Carotid Arteries (R and L)
Right arises from the brachiocephalic trunk; left arises directly from the aortic arch. Both ascend to bifurcate into internal and external carotids.
Subclavian Arteries (R and L)
Right arises from the brachiocephalic trunk; left arises directly from the aortic arch. Continue as axillary arteries.
Brachiocephalic Trunk
First and largest branch of the aortic arch; divides into the right common carotid and right subclavian arteries.
Aortic Arch
Connects the ascending and descending aorta over the heart; gives off the three great branches to the head and upper limbs.
Trachea
Visible behind the great vessels; bifurcates at the carina into right and left mainstem bronchi.
Right and Left Atria
Receive blood from the body (RA) and lungs (LA); auricles project anteriorly.
Right and Left Ventricles
Pump blood to the lungs (RV) and body (LV). RV forms the anterior surface; LV forms the apex and left lateral border.
Clinical Pearl
Why the Left Brachiocephalic Vein Crosses the Midline
The SVC sits on the right because the embryonic right anterior cardinal vein dominates while the left regresses. The left brachiocephalic vein is the only major remaining cross-connection from left to right, draining left jugular and subclavian blood across the midline to the SVC. Its long course makes it accessible from the left side for some central line placements but also means it can be compressed by anterior mediastinal masses.
Plate 07

Anterior Surface (Cadaveric)

On the cadaveric heart, the right ventricle dominates the anterior surface. The right auricle drapes over the ascending aorta on the right side.

Identify These Structures
Aorta
Ascending portion arising from the left ventricle, visible centrally as it emerges from behind the pulmonary trunk.
Pulmonary Trunk
Arises from the right ventricle and ascends anterior to the aorta before bifurcating into right and left pulmonary arteries.
Right Auricle
Ear-shaped flap of the right atrium, draping over the root of the aorta.
Right Atrium
Sits beneath the right auricle; receives the SVC superiorly and the IVC inferiorly.
Right Ventricle
Forms most of the anterior heart surface; lies between the apex and the AV groove.
Clinical Pearl
Why the Right Ventricle Bleeds First
In penetrating chest trauma (knife or low-velocity bullet wound), the right ventricle is the most commonly injured chamber simply because it sits most anteriorly, immediately behind the sternum and lower ribs. Anterior chest stab wounds with hemodynamic compromise warrant emergency department thoracotomy if the patient arrests in front of you, with right ventricular repair often being the lifesaving intervention.
Plate 08

Lateral Views: Left and Right Sides

From either side, the relationship of the heart to the bronchi, pulmonary vessels, and great vessels becomes clear. The ligamentum arteriosum, a fetal remnant, is visible on the left.

Identify These Structures
Ligamentum Arteriosum
Fibrous remnant of the fetal ductus arteriosus, connecting the aortic arch to the pulmonary trunk. The recurrent laryngeal nerve hooks under it on the left.
Left Pulmonary Artery
Branch of the pulmonary trunk; passes posteriorly toward the left lung.
Right Pulmonary Artery
Branch of the pulmonary trunk; passes posteriorly under the aortic arch toward the right lung.
Left Auricle and Atrium
Visible on the left side; receives the four pulmonary veins.
Great Cardiac Vein
Visible coursing along the left side of the heart toward the coronary sinus.
Left Ventricle
Forms the left lateral border and apex; thick myocardial wall visible from the side.
Aorta (Lateral View)
Visible curving over the left mainstem bronchus and descending posteriorly.
Left Mainstem Bronchus
Longer and more horizontal than the right; passes under the aortic arch.
Left Pulmonary Veins
Two veins draining the left lung into the left atrium.
Right Subclavian Artery and Vein
Visible exiting laterally from the great vessel cluster.
Superior Vena Cava (Right View)
Descending toward the right atrium.
Inferior Vena Cava
Returns blood from below the diaphragm; pierces the diaphragm at T8 to enter the right atrium.
Clinical Pearl
Patent Ductus Arteriosus
In fetal life, the ductus arteriosus shunts blood from the pulmonary trunk to the aorta, bypassing the unused lungs. Functional closure occurs within hours of birth as oxygen rises and prostaglandins fall; anatomic closure produces the ligamentum arteriosum within weeks. If the ductus remains patent (PDA), a continuous machinery murmur is heard at the left infraclavicular area, and a left-to-right shunt eventually leads to pulmonary hypertension. Indomethacin (a prostaglandin inhibitor) closes a PDA medically; alternatively, surgical or catheter closure is performed.
Plate 09

Posterior Surface and Pulmonary Veins

From behind, the left atrium dominates the heart base, with four pulmonary veins entering, two from each lung. The cadaveric and model views show the same arrangement.

Identify These Structures
Left Atrium
Forms most of the posterior surface (base) of the heart. Receives oxygenated blood from the four pulmonary veins.
Right Pulmonary Veins
Two veins (superior and inferior right) draining the right lung; enter the left atrium on its right side.
Left Pulmonary Veins
Two veins (superior and inferior left) draining the left lung; enter the left atrium on its left side.
Aorta (Posterior)
The arch and descending portion are visible posteriorly, curving over the left main bronchus.
Pulmonary Trunk
Bifurcates into right and left pulmonary arteries beneath the aortic arch.
Superior Vena Cava
Visible entering the right atrium from above on the model.
Inferior Vena Cava
Visible entering the right atrium from below on the model; pierces the diaphragm at T8.
Coronary Sinus
Large venous channel on the posterior surface in the coronary sulcus; receives most cardiac venous drainage and empties into the right atrium.
Clinical Pearl
Pulmonary Vein Foci and Atrial Fibrillation
Atrial fibrillation, the most common sustained arrhythmia, often originates from ectopic electrical foci near the ostia of the pulmonary veins as they enter the left atrium. Catheter ablation for atrial fibrillation, called pulmonary vein isolation, creates circumferential lesions around these openings to electrically disconnect the triggering tissue from the rest of the atrium.
Plate 10

Coronary Sulcus and Coronary Sinus

The coronary sulcus is the groove that encircles the heart at the AV junction. The coronary sinus is the venous channel that lies within its posterior portion before draining into the right atrium.

Identify These Structures
Coronary Sulcus
The atrioventricular groove encircling the heart, separating atria above from ventricles below. Contains coronary vessels.
Coronary Sinus
Large venous channel running in the posterior coronary sulcus. Collects most cardiac venous return and empties into the right atrium.
Inferior Vena Cava (Opening)
Large venous opening in the inferior aspect of the right atrium; returns deoxygenated blood from below the diaphragm.
Clinical Pearl
The Coronary Sinus and Cardiac Pacing
In cardiac resynchronization therapy (biventricular pacing), one pacing lead must reach the lateral wall of the left ventricle. Because direct LV access is too risky, electrophysiologists thread the lead retrograde through the coronary sinus and into a posterolateral cardiac vein, where it sits on the epicardial LV surface. The coronary sinus is also the route for retrograde cardioplegia during cardiac surgery.
Section 3

Coronary Vasculature

The arterial circulation that feeds the heart muscle itself.

Plate 11

Coronary Arteries

The two coronary arteries arise from the aortic root just above the aortic valve. The right coronary runs in the right AV groove; the left coronary divides into the LAD (anterior interventricular) and circumflex.

Identify These Structures
Right Coronary Artery (RCA)
Arises from the right aortic sinus; runs in the right coronary sulcus toward the posterior heart. Supplies the right atrium, right ventricle, SA node (in 60 percent), and AV node (in 90 percent).
Left Coronary Artery (Left Main)
Arises from the left aortic sinus; runs briefly behind the pulmonary trunk before dividing into the LAD and circumflex arteries.
Anterior Interventricular Artery (LAD)
Branch of the left coronary; runs in the anterior interventricular sulcus toward the apex. Supplies the anterior LV, anterior interventricular septum, and apex.
Circumflex Artery
Branch of the left coronary; runs in the left coronary sulcus around to the posterior heart. Supplies the lateral and posterior LV.
Great Cardiac Vein
Runs alongside the LAD in the anterior interventricular sulcus, then curves left in the coronary sulcus to drain into the coronary sinus.
Aorta (Coronary Origin)
The coronary arteries arise from the aortic root just superior to the aortic valve cusps; they are the first branches of the systemic circulation.
Pulmonary Trunk
Sits anterior to the aortic root; the left main coronary artery passes briefly behind it before bifurcating.
Right Auricle
Lies adjacent to the origin of the RCA, draping over the aortic root.
Left Auricle
Lies adjacent to the origin of the left coronary artery and the proximal circumflex.
Clinical Pearl
The LAD and the Widow Maker
Proximal LAD occlusion is nicknamed the "widow maker" because the LAD supplies the anterior wall, anterior septum, and apex of the left ventricle, the heart largest territory. A proximal LAD occlusion causes a massive anterior MI with high mortality from cardiogenic shock or ventricular arrhythmia. RCA occlusion in contrast causes inferior MI and can cause heart block (because the RCA supplies the AV node in 90 percent of people) but is generally better tolerated.
Section 4

Internal Anatomy and Conduction

Wall layers, valves, chambers from within, and the electrical highway of the heart.

Plate 12

Heart Wall Layers

The heart wall has three layers, named for their position relative to the cardiac muscle. A magnified inset shows the layers in cross-section.

Identify These Structures
Epicardium
Outermost layer. From Greek epi meaning "upon." Also called visceral pericardium; the inner layer of the serous pericardium. Contains the coronary vessels and a layer of fat.
Myocardium
Middle and thickest layer. From Greek myo meaning "muscle." Cardiac muscle responsible for contraction. Three to four times thicker in the left ventricle than the right.
Endocardium
Innermost layer. From Greek endo meaning "within." Smooth endothelial lining continuous with the inner lining of blood vessels; covers the valves, chordae tendineae, and trabeculae carneae.
Clinical Pearl
Inflammation by Layer
Each layer can become inflamed, producing distinct clinical syndromes. Pericarditis (often viral) causes sharp positional chest pain that worsens lying flat and improves leaning forward, plus a friction rub. Myocarditis causes heart failure, arrhythmia, and elevated troponins, often viral. Endocarditis (often bacterial, on damaged valves or prosthetics) causes vegetations, fever, embolic phenomena (Janeway lesions, Osler nodes), and a new murmur.
Plate 13

The Four Cardiac Valves

A horizontal section through the heart shows all four valves on the same plane. The two AV valves separate atria from ventricles; the two semilunar valves guard the great-vessel outflows.

Identify These Structures
Tricuspid Valve
Right atrioventricular valve with three cusps. Closes during ventricular systole to prevent backflow into the right atrium. Anchored by chordae tendineae to papillary muscles.
Bicuspid Valve (Mitral)
Left atrioventricular valve with two cusps. Named "mitral" because the cusps resemble a bishop miter. Closes during ventricular systole to prevent backflow into the left atrium.
Pulmonary Semilunar Valve (Pulmonic)
Three cusp valve at the base of the pulmonary trunk. Closes after right ventricular systole to prevent backflow from the pulmonary trunk into the right ventricle.
Aortic Semilunar Valve
Three cusp valve at the base of the aorta. Closes after left ventricular systole to prevent backflow from the aorta into the left ventricle. Coronary arteries arise from sinuses just above the cusps.
Clinical Pearl
Heart Sounds and Auscultation
S1 ("lub") is the closure of the AV valves (tricuspid and mitral) at the start of systole. S2 ("dub") is the closure of the semilunar valves (pulmonic and aortic) at the start of diastole. Auscultation areas (where each valve is best heard) are not over the valve itself but downstream where blood flows: aortic at right 2nd intercostal space, pulmonic at left 2nd, tricuspid at left lower sternal border, mitral at the apex (5th intercostal, midclavicular).
Plate 14

Right Atrium Interior

Opening the right atrium reveals the rough pectinate muscles, the smooth-walled posterior portion, the openings for venous return, and the fossa ovalis.

Identify These Structures
Right Atrium
Receives deoxygenated blood. Wall is divided into a smooth posterior portion (sinus venarum) derived from the embryonic sinus venosus and a rough anterior portion (auricle) marked by pectinate muscles.
Pectinate Muscles
Parallel ridges of muscle on the inner wall of the right auricle. Comb-like appearance gave them their name (pecten = comb).
Superior Vena Cava (Opening)
Opens into the upper posterior right atrium; returns blood from the upper body. No valve guards this opening.
Inferior Vena Cava (Opening)
Opens into the lower posterior right atrium; returns blood from below the diaphragm. Rudimentary Eustachian valve guards the opening.
Coronary Sinus (Opening)
Small opening in the posterior right atrium near the AV junction; returns most cardiac venous blood. Guarded by the rudimentary Thebesian valve.
Fossa Ovalis
Oval depression in the interatrial septum. Remnant of the fetal foramen ovale, which closed shortly after birth.
Tricuspid Valve (View)
Visible at the lower right of the chamber, leading to the right ventricle.
Pulmonary Veins (Behind)
Visible in the cadaveric view entering the left atrium behind the right atrium. The four pulmonary veins are the only veins that carry oxygenated blood.
Clinical Pearl
The Foramen Ovale and Cryptogenic Stroke
In fetal life, the foramen ovale is an opening in the interatrial septum that shunts oxygenated placental blood from right atrium to left atrium, bypassing the lungs. After birth, increased left atrial pressure usually closes the flap, leaving the fossa ovalis. In about 25 percent of adults, the flap remains incompletely fused (patent foramen ovale, PFO), allowing intermittent right-to-left shunt. PFO is implicated in cryptogenic stroke when a venous thrombus crosses to the systemic arterial circulation.
Plate 15

Pathway Through the Right Heart and SA Node

A sectioned model traces the path of deoxygenated blood from the SA node (where each heartbeat begins) through the right atrium, tricuspid valve, and into the right ventricle.

Identify These Structures
Sinoatrial (SA) Node
The hearts natural pacemaker. A small region of specialized conduction tissue in the upper right atrial wall, near the SVC entry. Generates each heartbeat by spontaneous depolarization.
Superior Vena Cava (Entry)
Entry point of the SVC into the right atrium, just adjacent to the SA node.
Right Atrium
Receives blood from SVC, IVC, and coronary sinus. Smooth posterior wall and rough anterior wall (pectinate muscles).
Pectinate Muscles
Visible ridges on the auricular wall.
Opening to Inferior Vena Cava
Returns deoxygenated blood from below the diaphragm.
Opening to Coronary Sinus
Returns deoxygenated blood from the heart wall itself.
Tricuspid Valve
Three cusp AV valve. Opens during diastole to allow RA blood to flow into the RV; closes during systole.
Chordae Tendineae
Tendinous cords connecting the tricuspid cusps to papillary muscles in the RV. Prevent valve prolapse during systole.
Right Ventricle
Receives blood from the RA via the tricuspid valve. Pumps blood out through the pulmonic valve to the pulmonary trunk.
Clinical Pearl
The SA Node and Sick Sinus Syndrome
The SA node fires at an intrinsic rate, modulated by autonomic tone (sympathetic accelerates, parasympathetic slows). With age and ischemia, the SA node can fail, producing inappropriate bradycardia, sinus pauses, or alternating bradycardia and tachycardia (tachy-brady syndrome). Treatment is permanent pacemaker implantation. The SA node receives blood from the SA nodal artery, a branch of the RCA in 60 percent of people and the circumflex in 40 percent, which is why right coronary occlusion can produce sinus bradycardia.
Plate 16

Tricuspid Valve Close-Up

A closer view of the tricuspid valve from the right atrial side shows the three cusps that prevent backflow during ventricular contraction.

Identify These Structures
Tricuspid Valve
Right atrioventricular valve with three cusps (anterior, posterior, septal). Closes during ventricular systole to prevent backflow into the right atrium.
Right Atrium
The chamber above the tricuspid valve. Blood flows from here into the right ventricle when the valve opens during diastole.
Clinical Pearl
Tricuspid Valve and IV Drug Use Endocarditis
Right-sided endocarditis is much rarer than left-sided endocarditis in the general population, but it is the most common type in people who inject drugs. Pathogens, often Staphylococcus aureus, enter the venous circulation and lodge first on the tricuspid valve. Septic emboli from the tricuspid valve travel to the lungs, producing multiple pulmonary infiltrates that look like cannonball lesions on chest imaging.
Plate 17

Left Atrium Interior

The left atrium opened from behind reveals the four pulmonary vein openings entering the smooth-walled chamber. Pectinate muscles, abundant in the right atrium, are restricted to the auricle here.

Identify These Structures
Left Atrium
Receives oxygenated blood from the four pulmonary veins. Posterior wall is smooth; pectinate muscles are confined to the LA appendage (auricle).
Right Pulmonary Vein Openings
Two openings in the right side of the posterior LA wall (superior and inferior right pulmonary veins). Drain the right lung.
Left Pulmonary Vein Openings
Two openings in the left side of the posterior LA wall (superior and inferior left pulmonary veins). Drain the left lung.
Pectinate Muscles (LA Auricle)
Visible inside the left auricle on adjacent dissection. The bulk of the LA posterior wall is smooth.
Left Coronary Artery (Cut)
Visible exiting the LA wall in cross-section near the AV junction.
Pulmonary Artery (Behind)
Visible posterior to the left atrium in this view.
Clinical Pearl
The Left Atrial Appendage and Stroke Risk
The LA appendage (auricle) is a finger-like outpouching with a narrow neck. In atrial fibrillation, blood pools in this trabeculated chamber and can form thrombi that embolize systemically, most disastrously to the brain. Anticoagulation with warfarin or DOACs prevents this. In patients who cannot tolerate anticoagulation, the appendage can be occluded mechanically (Watchman device) or surgically removed at the time of cardiac surgery.
Plate 18

Internal Ventricle: Chordae and Papillary Muscles

The ventricular interior reveals the AV valve apparatus: papillary muscles project from the wall, with chordae tendineae anchoring the valve cusps to prevent prolapse during contraction.

Identify These Structures
Chordae Tendineae
Tendinous cords (heart strings) connecting AV valve cusps to papillary muscles. Prevent the valve cusps from prolapsing back into the atrium during ventricular contraction.
Papillary Muscles
Cone-shaped muscle projections from the ventricular wall. Anchor the chordae tendineae. Contract just before the ventricle to tense the chordae and stabilize the valve.
Endocardium
Innermost layer of the heart wall. Smooth endothelial lining continuous with the inner lining of blood vessels; covers the valves and chordae.
Myocardium
Middle and thickest layer; cardiac muscle responsible for contraction. Much thicker in the left ventricle than the right.
Epicardium (Visceral Pericardium)
Outer layer of the heart wall and the inner layer of the serous pericardium. Contains the coronary vessels and a layer of fat.
Clinical Pearl
Papillary Muscle Rupture After MI
A myocardial infarction involving the papillary muscle, most often the posteromedial papillary muscle of the mitral valve, can cause papillary muscle rupture 3 to 7 days after the event. The result is sudden severe mitral regurgitation with flash pulmonary edema and cardiogenic shock. The posteromedial papillary muscle is more vulnerable because it has a single blood supply (posterior descending artery), unlike the anterolateral papillary muscle which has dual supply (LAD and circumflex). Surgical emergency.
Plate 19

All Four Chambers Comprehensive

A coronal section through the heart with the chambers folded open exposes all four chambers, both AV valves, the pulmonic valve, and the muscular walls in a single view.

Identify These Structures
Right Atrium
Upper right chamber. Receives systemic venous return.
Right Ventricle
Lower right chamber. Pumps blood through the pulmonic valve to the pulmonary trunk.
Left Atrium
Upper left chamber. Receives oxygenated blood from the pulmonary veins.
Left Ventricle
Lower left chamber. Pumps blood through the aortic valve to the aorta. Wall is roughly three times thicker than the right ventricle.
Superior Vena Cava
Major venous return entering the upper RA.
Aorta
Exits the LV via the aortic valve; first branches are the coronary arteries.
Pulmonary Trunk
Exits the RV via the pulmonic valve; bifurcates to right and left pulmonary arteries.
Pulmonic Valve
Three cusp semilunar valve at the base of the pulmonary trunk.
Pectinate Muscles
Ridges of muscle on the inner wall of the atria, especially the right auricle.
Trabeculae Carneae
Irregular muscular ridges on the inner walls of both ventricles. Distinct from the smooth-walled atrial sinus venarum portion.
Papillary Muscle
Cone-shaped muscle projections inside both ventricles; anchor the chordae tendineae.
Clinical Pearl
Wall Thickness and Pressure
The left ventricular wall is about three times thicker than the right (roughly 10 to 12 mm vs. 3 to 5 mm) because it pumps against systemic pressure (around 120 mmHg) while the right pumps against pulmonary pressure (around 25 mmHg). Right ventricular hypertrophy develops in chronic pulmonary hypertension, and on imaging the RV wall thickens until it approaches LV dimensions, a hallmark of cor pulmonale.
Plate 20

Cardiac Conduction System

A specialized network of cells generates and propagates the electrical impulse for each heartbeat, traveling from the SA node through the AV node, bundle of His, bundle branches, and Purkinje fibers.

Identify These Structures
Sinoatrial (SA) Node
Pacemaker of the heart. Located in the upper right atrium near the SVC. Intrinsic rate 60 to 100 beats per minute. Modulated by autonomic input.
Atrioventricular (AV) Node
Located in the floor of the right atrium near the coronary sinus opening. Delays the impulse before it reaches the ventricles, allowing atrial contraction to complete. Intrinsic rate 40 to 60 bpm.
Bundle of His (AV Bundle)
Carries the impulse from the AV node into the interventricular septum. The only normal electrical bridge from atria to ventricles. Intrinsic rate 40 to 60 bpm.
Right Bundle Branch
Descends along the right side of the interventricular septum to the right ventricle. Conducts the impulse only; no intrinsic pacemaker rate.
Left Bundle Branch
Descends along the left side of the interventricular septum to the left ventricle. Splits into anterior and posterior fascicles. Conducts the impulse only.
Purkinje Fibers
Network of fibers terminating in the ventricular myocardium. Spread the impulse rapidly throughout the ventricular muscle for synchronous contraction. Intrinsic rate 20 to 40 bpm.
Clinical Pearl
Heart Block
When AV node conduction fails, the ventricles fall back on a lower pacemaker. First-degree block: PR interval prolonged, all impulses conducted. Second-degree (Mobitz I): PR progressively lengthens until a beat is dropped. Second-degree (Mobitz II): unpredictable dropped beats, often progresses to complete block. Third-degree (complete): no atrial impulses reach the ventricles, which beat at 20 to 40 bpm from a ventricular escape rhythm. Mobitz II and complete heart block need pacemakers.
Colophon
